div.p: C Y R O G R A P H

To all the sons of holy Mother Church who shall see or hear the present document, Gilbert, by the grace of God, bishop of Dunkeld, and John and Philip, by the same grace, humble abbots of Lindores and Scone, wish eternal salvation in Christ. Inasmuch as a dispute was moved between the venerable father, the lord Clement bishop of Dunblane, on the one side, and the lord abbot Innocent, and the convent of Inchaffray, on the other side, over the churches of Aberuthven and Tullichettle, and over the teinds coming from rents and all manner of perquisites of the lord earl of Strathearn, which are called second teinds, all which the said bishop claimed, saying that they pertained to his table by reason of his church; at length, on the advice of worthy men, the said bishop, for himself and his successors (with the consent and assent of the whole clergy of Dunblane, to whom the election of the bishop belongs, also with the assent of the lord Robert, earl of Strathern, patron of the same see), and the said abbot and convent, for themselves and their successors, agreed to submit to our arbitration on the aforesaid articles, as also on all other churches and possessions at that time conferred and granted to them, with the addition of the following penalty, that if the same bishop or any of his successors desired to withdraw from our decision, in the first place, if any advantage should come to his church through our decision, he should restore it to the opposite party, and also pay to the fabric of the bridge of Perth two hundred pounds sterling by way of penalty before entering on litigation. And if the abbot and convent or their successors should wish to withdraw from our decision, they shall pay to the same bridge, by way of penalty, before entering on litigation three hundred pounds. The parties, therefore, having appeared in our presence, and the pleadings of each party having been heard and carefully considered, at length, on the advice of those learned in the law, we made the following ordinance, namely, that the said bishop was to abandon the whole action which he was raising, and renounce all right which he sought, or believed that he had, in respect to the matters aforesaid; and that the said abbot and convent was to assign from certain places to the said bishop and his successors, in the name of the church of Dunblane, an annual payment of sixteen pounds sterling, as reckoned by the judgment of worthy men. The parties accepting this our award, to the end that it might obtain the strength of perpetual validity, asked that it might be reduced to writing in the manner of a cyrograph, of which one part sealed with our seals, together with the seal of the said bishop, should remain at the aforesaid monastery of Inchaffray; and the other part sealed similarly with our seals and the seal of the chapter of the same monastery, should remain with the said bishop. Given in the 1234th year since the Incarnation of the Lord, in the month of August.
